Only 'approval forward' is implicit. Missing: (1) Withdraw authorization (reverse F18 approval), (2) Re-open closed trial, (3) Rollback to previous version. These are essential CRUD operations for reversibility.
findings.recommendation: Define three missing features: (1) Authorization Withdrawal, (2) Trial Re-opening, (3) Case Revision Rollback. Clarify: (1) what triggers these actions, (2) who can initiate them, (3) what audit trail is created, (4) what happens to associated artefacts.

findings.finding_title: Swarm SORA and Swarm Decision-Making Features Overlap
findings.feature_name: SORA 2.0 Assessment (F03) vs Swarm Decision-Making Architecture Review (F09)
findings.business_story: Both features require evidence of swarm collective behaviour; F03 (SORA) focuses on risk quantification, F09 (architecture) focuses on decision-making logic.
findings.reasoning: REQ002 (F03 SORA) and REQ008 (F09 architecture) both require 'swarm evidence'. SORA needs swarm risk models and emergent-behaviour test data. Architecture review needs decision-making logic and emergent-behaviour evidence. The input artefacts likely overlap (same test data, same behaviour models). Unclear whether F03 and F09 are independent workflows or sequential.
findings.recommendation: Clarify dependency and artifact sharing: (1) Does F03 consume outputs from F09, or are they independent? (2) Which features own the 'emergent behaviour evidence'? (3) Are swarm risk models generated by F09 and consumed by F03, or are they separate? Define clear boundaries to avoid duplicate work.

findings.finding_title: Granularity Mismatch: Epic vs Feature vs Sub-feature
findings.business_story: Some REQs describe broad epics (e.g., F03 SORA, F09 swarm decision), others describe specific tasks (e.g., F14 insurance upload, F16 crew competency).
findings.reasoning: REQ002 (SORA) spans multiple assessment sub-tasks and integrates evidence from multiple sources. REQ014 (crew training) is a single, focused review task. REQ013 (insurance + ground survey) combines two unrelated tasks. This inconsistency makes it difficult to estimate scope, plan sprints, or define test strategy. Some REQs are epics, others are features, others are sub-features.
findings.recommendation: Apply consistent granularity criteria: (1) Define feature size (e.g., 'can be completed in 1-2 sprints by one team'), (2) Re-scope large REQs (REQ002, REQ008, REQ009) as epics with explicitly named features, (3) Group small REQs (REQ013, REQ014) where appropriate. Use a feature-size rubric (story points, person-days) to normalize.

findings.finding_title: Panel Decision Override Logging Not Explicit
findings.business_story: As a CAA panel member I want to log overrides and decisions so that all decision points are audited and reversals are traceable.
findings.reasoning: REQ016 describes 'reversible' gates but does not specify how reversals are logged or what happens when a panel reverses a previous decision. This is critical for audit trails and regulatory defensibility. Who logs the reversal? What rationale is captured? How is the audit trail protected from tampering?
findings.recommendation: Define F26 (Panel Override & Decision Logging). Clarify: (1) what system actions trigger override logging, (2) what data is captured (actor, timestamp, rationale, prior state), (3) who can initiate overrides, (4) what audit trail is immutable, (5) how overrides are reflected in case history.

findings.finding_title: REQ020: Post-trial close-out failure outcomes undefined (NO USER STORY)
findings.outcome_description: REQ020 is not a user story but a NOTE: 'Post-Trial Close-out' is 'absent' and only mentions 'lessons-learned, evidence preservation, close-out'. The requirement does not specify what happens if post-trial archival FAILS (evidence lost, lessons-learned not completed, authorization not formally closed).
findings.triggering_scenario: trial execution is complete; system must transition from active authorization to closed/archived state
findings.reasoning: REQ020 is important for institutional learning and regulatory audit trail but is absent a user story. Without explicit close-out outcomes, the trial may drift into an indeterminate state (active or closed?). This is a gap for operational and regulatory clarity.
findings.recommendation: Create explicit REQ020 user story: 'As a CAA programme manager, I want to close-out trial authorization so that lessons are captured and evidence is preserved.' Define close-out outcomes: (1) upon trial termination (end of license period or early termination), system enters close-out phase; (2) applicant submits post-trial report (flights flown, incidents, lessons learned, unexpected behaviors); (3) all trial evidence (flights logs, incident reports, swarm behavior data) is archived to immutable storage with retention policy (minimum 7 years); (4) CAA reviews close-out report and final evidence; (5) authorization is formally CLOSED (no new flights permitted); (6) case file is sealed and moved to archive; (7) if close-out data is missing, case remains OPEN pending completion. Define close-out checklist and archive retention policy.

findings.finding_title: REQ005-REQ011: Conflicting outcomes - containment enforcement vs independent termination overlap
findings.outcome_description: REQ005 (Geofence enforcement) and REQ011 (Flight termination in off-nominal state) both trigger on boundary/failure conditions, but the relationship is unclear. If geofence breach is detected, does containment enforcement (REQ005) execute, or does independent termination (REQ011) execute? Both specify outcome (containment / termination) but not the priority or interaction.
findings.triggering_scenario: swarm aircraft breaches geofence AND simultaneously detects loss of C2 link
findings.reasoning: Geofence enforcement and flight termination are overlapping safety mechanisms. REQ005 says 'containment prevents fly-away'; REQ011 says 'independent termination makes trial safe in off-nominal state'. But if geofence AND link loss occur simultaneously, which mechanism takes priority? Can both trigger (double-termination)? Is there a conflict?
findings.recommendation: Define interaction outcomes for REQ005 and REQ011: (1) geofence breach -> triggers containment enforcement (soft stop, hold position, crew alert); (2) if containment engagement fails (aircraft continues ascending despite motor reduction), escalate to independent termination within 10 seconds; (3) link loss -> triggers autonomous RTL (pre-planned return path) or hold altitude; (4) if autonomous RTL fails (onboard navigation loss), escalate to independent termination; (5) independent termination is the ultimate fallback (last resort) for any off-nominal state. Define failure detection and escalation chain.

findings.finding_title: REQ006 'Link loss survivable' lacks quantitative time-to-recovery threshold
findings.scenario_text: Given: C2 link is lost. When: Swarm must survive loss without collision or uncontrolled flight. Then: System behavior depends on hold-safe timeout, but threshold (10s? 30s?) is not specified.
findings.reasoning: REQ006 uses 'link loss is survivable' but Dorothy's glossary flags this as unverified: 'what is survivable? defined by mission, location, or crew capability?'. Time-to-recovery directly impacts operational envelope (e.g., high-altitude flight requires faster RTL than low-altitude). Without explicit threshold, SORA assessment and contingency design are subjective.
findings.recommendation: Define link-loss survivability threshold: 'C2 link loss for duration < [10 seconds] is survivable via inter-vehicle links and pre-planned autonomy. Loss > [10 seconds] triggers RTL to nearest safe landing zone. Justification: swarm inertial/vision navigation can maintain altitude ±[50m] for [10s] with inter-vehicle ranging." Validate threshold through simulation and flight test.

findings.finding_title: REQ012 'Complete recorded evidence' lacks data type and sampling rate specification
findings.feature: F13: Incident Data Recording & MOR Handling
findings.scenario_text: Given: Incident investigation depends on recorded evidence. When: Investigator analyzes post-flight data. Then: Completeness is ambiguous—what data types? What sampling rate? What fidelity is sufficient?
findings.reasoning: REQ012 requires 'complete recorded evidence' but Dorothy's glossary notes this is unverified: 'sampling rates, data types, retention period undefined.' Without explicit data specification (e.g., '100 Hz IMU, 10 Hz GPS, 1 Hz C2 link status'), investigator may find evidence insufficient for root-cause analysis.
findings.recommendation: Define data recording specification: (1) Aircraft state: IMU [100 Hz], GPS [10 Hz], barometer [10 Hz]; (2) Autonomy decisions: decision log [1 Hz] with decision type, inputs, outputs; (3) C2 link: link status [10 Hz], command/response pairs [sampled]; (4) Crew actions: control input [10 Hz], voice communication [audio]; (5) Retention: minimum [30 days] for non-incident flights, [7 years] for incident flights. Validate sufficiency through post-flight incident simulations.

findings.finding_title: REQ015 'Gap-free' apportionment lacks verification method - no audit trail specified
findings.feature: F17: CAA/MAA Apportionment & Duty Holder Signature
findings.scenario_text: Given: Apportionment must be 'gap-free' with no overlap or omission. When: Apportionment document is signed. Then: Verification method (how to detect gaps?) is undefined.
findings.reasoning: REQ015 requires 'gap-free' apportionment but Dorothy's glossary notes this is unverified: 'criterion for completeness undefined; what constitutes a gap? How is it verified?'. Without explicit verification checklist, hidden gaps (e.g., classified evidence custody unassigned) may remain undetected.
findings.recommendation: Define gap-free verification checklist: (1) List all regulatory domains covered by trial: airspace, spectrum, safety, security, environment, training, insurance, data protection; (2) For each domain, verify exactly one authority (CAA or MAA) is assigned; (3) Document any shared responsibility explicitly (e.g., 'classified evidence review: CAA safety assessor (with MAA liaison)'); (4) Trace each REQ to responsible authority (no orphaned REQs); (5) Audit trail of signatures confirming both parties reviewed and agreed.

findings.finding_title: REQ016 Phase gates lack explicit pass/fail criteria - evidence thresholds are undefined
findings.feature: F18: Phased Trial Gates & Progressive Evidence Build
findings.scenario_text: Given: Phase gate determines whether trial can progress. When: Panel reviews evidence. Then: Acceptance criteria ('evidence sufficient?') are subjective—what is the threshold?
findings.reasoning: REQ016 requires 'phased gates' for progressive build-up but does not specify gate pass/fail criteria. Dorothy's glossary notes: 'number, timing, and success criteria for each gate undefined.' Without explicit criteria (e.g., 'phase pass requires 10 incident-free flights + swarm behavior nominal in all autonomy modes'), gate decisions are subjective and reproducibility is poor.
findings.recommendation: Define phase gate criteria (see SCN-060 data-driven scenario). For each phase, specify: (1) Evidence portfolio (flight logs, autonomy behavior logs, test results); (2) Quantitative pass threshold (e.g., 10 flights, zero incidents, autonomy behavior within ODD bounds); (3) Review process (independent panel, criteria checklist); (4) Documentation (gate review report with signed approval or rejection rationale). Implement gate criteria in system checklist.

findings.finding_title: REQ007: DAA low confidence scenario outcome undefined - what happens when detection confidence is low
findings.scenario: GIVEN DAA system detects potential intruder with LOW confidence (sensor ambiguity, intermittent track) WHEN intruder confidence score is <70% THEN [outcome undefined: escalate as threat? dismiss? escalate to crew?]
findings.predicted_outcome: DAA applies confidence threshold: if intruder confidence >80%, treat as definite threat and execute avoidance; if 60-80%, alert crew and allow crew to decide (escalate, dismiss, or override); if <60%, log detection but do not escalate or maneuver
findings.realistic_alternative: DAA treats all detections equally regardless of confidence; all detected targets trigger equivalent avoidance maneuvers; crew is overwhelmed with false positive alerts
findings.absurd_alternative: DAA only acts on high-confidence targets; low-confidence potential collisions are ignored; if low-confidence target becomes high-confidence mid-maneuver (suddenly clear track), avoidance is late; collision risk is unmitigated
findings.reasoning: Real-world sensor systems (radar, vision) produce detections with varying confidence scores. REQ007 requires encounters 'are resolved predictably' but does not address confidence-weighted decision logic. A developer must hardcode thresholds for treating ambiguous detections. Without specification, two developers might implement opposite strategies: fail-safe (low-confidence treated as threat) or fail-open (low-confidence ignored). Prediction is impossible.
findings.recommendation: Add to REQ007: 'DAA system shall apply confidence-weighted response to intruder detections. (1) All detections are assigned confidence score (0-100%) based on sensor agreement and track stability. (2) Confidence >85%: definite threat, execute autonomous avoidance immediately. (3) Confidence 70-85%: likely threat, alert crew with 'CAUTION: Possible intruder [confidence]%'; crew has 5-second window to confirm/dismiss; if crew does not respond, escalate to autonomous avoidance. (4) Confidence <70%: ambiguous detection, log to black box and display to crew as 'Possible target' advisory; no maneuver unless crew selects avoidance. (5) If ambiguous detection is confirmed post-maneuver, high-confidence follow-up actions are enabled. Define confidence scoring method and thresholds.'

findings.finding_title: REQ005-REQ011: Precedence undefined between geofence enforcement and flight termination
findings.scenario: GIVEN swarm aircraft approaches geofence boundary AND simultaneously detects off-nominal state (e.g., structural failure) WHEN both geofence enforcement and flight termination are triggered THEN [outcome undefined: which executes first? can both execute?]
findings.predicted_outcome: Precedence is defined: geofence enforcement (soft-stop, containment) executes first to prevent boundary escape; if containment fails within 10 seconds, escalate to independent termination system. Independent termination is ultimate fallback (last resort).
findings.realistic_alternative: Both systems execute simultaneously; dual motor cutoff occurs; aircraft enters uncontrolled descent; independent termination redundancy is lost because both primary and backup systems fired together
findings.absurd_alternative: Flight termination takes precedence over geofence enforcement; aircraft outside boundary is immediately terminated rather than contained; boundary breach is tolerated because termination occurred; regulatory gap emerges (containment was required but overridden)
findings.reasoning: REQ005 (containment enforcement) and REQ011 (flight termination) are overlapping safety mechanisms. Both are triggered by off-nominal conditions. But the interaction/precedence between them is undefined. If both trigger simultaneously, which takes priority? Is there an escalation sequence? Two developers might implement: (1) containment first, then escalate; (2) termination as immediate backup; (3) parallel execution (both firing simultaneously). Prediction of safety behavior is impossible without explicit precedence.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'Safety system precedence and escalation shall be defined explicitly. (1) When multiple off-nominal triggers are detected (geofence breach AND structural failure detected), safety system applies escalation sequence: (a) LEVEL 1: Containment enforcement (soft-stop, hold position) attempts to prevent boundary breach; (b) if containment is ineffective (aircraft still escaping after soft-stop), escalate within 10 seconds; (c) LEVEL 2: Independent termination system activates (motors cut, controlled descent). (2) Crew is alerted at each escalation level with reason code. (3) Precedence is always: prevent boundary escape first, then mitigate structural hazard. Define escalation timings and sensor-confirmation logic.'

findings.finding_title: 'Clear ConOps and ODD'—clarity criteria undefined; audience unclear
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA safety-case reviewer I want a clear ConOps and ODD so that I can judge nominal and worst-credible behaviour. (REQ003)
findings.interpretation_a: 'Clear' means the reviewer can understand the document without domain expertise
findings.interpretation_b: 'Clear' means the document contains all information necessary for a domain expert to judge the stated behaviours (reviewer assumed expert)
findings.reasoning: Clarity is subjective and audience-dependent. A ConOps clear to an aerospace engineer may be unclear to a procurement specialist. Dorothy glossary notes 'clear' is a vague quantifier with no audience specified. The reviewer's assessment of clarity cannot be verified or objectified.
findings.recommendation: Replace 'clear' with specific quality criteria: 'A ConOps and ODD that conforms to [standard template], includes [sections X, Y, Z], defines all acronyms on first use, and provides a formal specification of nominal and worst-credible behaviour boundaries using [quantitative metrics / decision trees / state diagrams].'

findings.finding_title: Distinction between 'nominal' and 'worst-credible' behaviour undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA safety-case reviewer I want a clear ConOps and ODD so that I can judge nominal and worst-credible behaviour. (REQ003)
findings.interpretation_a: Nominal = expected performance under normal conditions; worst-credible = most severe foreseeable failure with quantifiable probability threshold (e.g., > 1e-6 / flight hour)
findings.interpretation_b: Nominal = planned behaviour; worst-credible = any adverse condition that could plausibly occur, with no quantitative boundary
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'worst-credible' as unverified with ambiguous boundary: 'what is credible vs incredible? Who judges this? No quantitative threshold given.' Without a quantitative boundary (e.g., failure rate, frequency), the reviewer cannot determine which failure modes must be covered. This creates regulatory inconsistency.
findings.recommendation: Define worst-credible behaviour with explicit criteria: 'Failure modes with failure rate >= [10^-6 | 10^-7 / flight hour] or those identified in DO-178C or similar aviation safety standard.' Alternatively, provide a formal credibility assessment method (e.g., expert panel, historical data).

findings.finding_title: 'Airborne limits'—type and setter undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA autonomy reviewer I want the decision-making architecture airborne limits and emergent-behaviour evidence so that I can assure what the swarm will do collectively. (REQ008)
findings.interpretation_a: Hard constraints on autonomous decisions (e.g., max altitude, speed); set by autonomy designer, enforced onboard
findings.interpretation_b: Soft advisory limits on crew decisions; set by regulator during authorization; monitored by system but not enforced
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'airborne limits' as unverified and 'ambiguous: are these hard constraints, soft limits, or advisory bounds? Who sets them?' Hard vs. soft distinction is critical for autonomy assurance. Different limit types imply different failure modes and recovery procedures. The autonomy reviewer cannot assess assurance without knowing limit mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Specify airborne limits with type and authority: 'The swarm autonomous decision-making shall be constrained by [hard limits | soft limits | advisory limits] on: [altitude, airspeed, turn rate, acceleration]. These limits shall be [set by autonomy designer and embedded in code | set by CAA during authorization and enforced by [monitor | enforcement module]]. Hard limits shall be [immutable during flight | modifiable only post-flight]. Soft limits shall trigger [alert | mode-down | crew intervention].'

findings.finding_title: 'Independent termination'—independence scope undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA flight-ops inspector I want independent termination a contingency matrix and ATSU coordination so that the trial can be made safe in any off-nominal state. (REQ011)
findings.interpretation_a: Termination function that operates independently of C2, crew, and onboard autonomy (three independent channels)
findings.interpretation_b: Termination function independent of normal flight control (but may share power, avionics, or data with other systems)
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'independent termination' as unverified: 'Independence criteria and test methods undefined. Relationship to containment unclear.' Independence from what exactly? C2 link? Crew commands? Autonomy system? Power supply? All three? How independent (separate hardware? separate power? architectural isolation?)? The relationship to 'automatic containment' (REQ005) is unclear: are these redundant, complementary, or mutually exclusive?
findings.recommendation: Define independent termination: 'Flight termination shall be independent of: (1) C2 link (no ground commands required), (2) Onboard autonomy system, (3) Main power system (fail-safe timer or battery-backed system). Independence shall be verified by [fault injection testing | safety analysis per DO-178C Level A]. Termination shall be triggered by [geofence breach, loss of C2 for > X seconds, autonomy watchdog timeout]. Relationship to containment (REQ005): [Containment prevents exit; Termination ensures safe landing upon exit].'

findings.finding_title: 'Factually grounded' third-party risk—evidence standard undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA authorisation officer I want insurance and a ground survey so that third-party risk is both transferable and factually grounded. (REQ013)
findings.interpretation_a: Risk assessment based on measured ground conditions (survey data, actual population density, observed infrastructure)
findings.interpretation_b: Risk assessment grounded in probabilistic model calibrated to empirical data (historical accident rates, validated simulation)
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'factually grounded' as unverified: 'Qualitative criterion. Factually implies verification standard but method undefined.' Different reviewers may judge what counts as 'fact'—surveyed ground data, historical UAS accident statistics, simulation predictions. The standard is discipline-dependent (insurance may trust actuarial data; CAA may demand direct observation; engineers may use models).
findings.recommendation: Define factually grounded with explicit evidence: 'Third-party risk shall be factually grounded via: (1) Documented ground survey [date, aerial imagery, population density map, infrastructure assessment per CAP 722 Section X], (2) Risk calculation [per SORA model | per [insurance standard]], (3) Validation [comparison to historical accident data | peer expert review]. Risk shall be documented as [failure rate in accidents/flight-hour], not as qualitative assessment.'

findings.finding_title: 'Evidenced competency'—evidence type and domain undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA licensing inspector I want evidenced competency so that the trial is flown by qualified crew. (REQ014)
findings.interpretation_a: Competency evidenced through existing UAS BVLOS licenses, supplemented by swarm-specific training and sign-off
findings.interpretation_b: Competency evidenced through new qualification exam or simulator evaluation specific to autonomous swarms
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'evidenced competency' as unverified: 'Evidence types and standards for BVLOS swarm operations undefined. Existing licenses adequate or new qualification needed?' Swarm operations introduce novel decision-making (distributed control, inter-vehicle comms failure, emergent behaviour) not covered in standard BVLOS licensing. Which evidence counts? Logbook hours in manned aviation? Prior BVLOS experience? Simulator performance? No standard exists for swarm competency assessment.
findings.recommendation: Specify competency evidence: 'Crew competency shall be evidenced via: (1) [Existing BVLOS license per CAP 722, category: [ACCA-Level 1 | Level 2]], (2) Swarm-specific qualification: [written exam ≥ X% pass rate | simulator evaluation ≥ Y scenarios at ≥ Z% success] per [training curriculum], (3) Endorsement signature from [type rating instructor | CAA examiner]. Competency domains: [basic airmanship | swarm dynamics | autonomy limits | contingency management]. Recurrent training: [annually | per incident].'

findings.finding_title: 'Signed apportionment' and 'gap-free'—signatory and gap criteria undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA/MAA liaison I want a signed apportionment so that regulatory ownership is gap-free. (REQ015)
findings.interpretation_a: Signed by CAA and MAA (Ministry of Aviation Authority or similar military authority); gap-free means every aspect assigned to one authority
findings.interpretation_b: Signed by CAA and co-regulator (MOD/MAA); gap-free means no overlap or ambiguity in decision authority
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'apportionment' as unverified: 'Specific allocation between CAA and MAA undefined. Document form and approval process unknown.' 'Gap-free' is undefined: what constitutes a gap? Overlapping jurisdiction? Conflicting requirements? Unclear decision authority? The signatory authority is ambiguous (CAA yes, but MAA is not defined). The document is implied but format not specified.
findings.recommendation: Specify apportionment: 'A signed Apportionment Document shall be agreed between [CAA and MOD/MAA], assigning regulatory authority over: (1) BVLOS authorization and ongoing compliance [CAA], (2) Classified MOD evidence handling and security assurance [MOD/MAA], (3) [Other domains]. The document shall be gap-free, meaning: [each function assigned to exactly one authority | overlapping authority resolved by [joint approval threshold]]. Apportionment shall be signed by [CAA Head of Delegation and MOD/MAA Head of Delegation]. Format: [form per CAA template | Apportionment Matrix]. Review trigger: [annually | upon trial scope change].'

findings.finding_title: 'Phased gates' and 'reversible'—phase definitions and reversal triggers undefined
findings.text_excerpt: As a CAA programme manager I want phased gates so that evidence builds progressively and is reversible. (REQ016)
findings.interpretation_a: Fixed sequence of 3-5 phases (e.g., single-aircraft, 2-aircraft, swarm); reversal triggered only on incident or safety finding
findings.interpretation_b: Flexible phases defined per trial profile; reversal possible at any gate; progression criteria not pre-determined
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'phased gates' as unverified: 'Number, timing, and success criteria for each gate undefined. Who approves progression?' 'Reversible' is unverified: 'Triggers and procedures for reversal undefined.' Without defined phases and approval criteria, the trial build-up is effectively unmanaged. Progression could happen ad-hoc or be delayed indefinitely. Reversal could be triggered by minor issues or avoided despite major findings.
findings.recommendation: Define phased gates: 'Phased trial build-up shall include [3 | 4 | 5] gates: Phase 1 [single aircraft, VLOS/EVLOS range]; Phase 2 [single aircraft, BVLOS]; Phase 3 [2-3 aircraft, coordinated]; Phase 4 [full swarm N aircraft, full autonomy]. Each phase shall progress upon [CAA approval based on: incident-free X flight-hours, evidence of [competency test | systems validation | scenario testing]]. Phase approval decision made by [CAA Programme Manager with [subject-matter expert review | independent review board]]. Reversal triggers: [incident with > severity Y | safety finding with | crew competency failure]. Reversal process: [grounded until root cause resolved | repeat phase gate].'

findings.finding_title: Potential contradiction: 'meaningful human control throughout' vs. 'autonomous swarm decision-making'
findings.text_excerpt: REQ010 requires 'meaningful human control...demonstrated throughout'; REQ008 requires autonomy reviewer to 'assure what the swarm will do collectively' (implying autonomous decision-making). Both cannot be fully satisfied if crew control is meaningful and continuous.
findings.interpretation_a: Crew maintain continuous override but delegation of decision-making to autonomy is permitted within pre-defined bounds
findings.interpretation_b: Crew have override capability but delegate most decisions to autonomy; control is meaningful but exercised selectively
findings.reasoning: REQ010 uses 'throughout', implying continuous control. REQ008 and REQ005 describe autonomous swarm decision-making and automatic containment, implying decentralized autonomous control. These positions are in tension: full human control at all times is incompatible with autonomous swarm operations; autonomous swarms limit human meaningful control. The requirements do not resolve this contradiction.
findings.recommendation: Clarify the autonomy model: 'The swarm shall operate under [shared control model]: (1) Crew authorize mission phases and set high-level goals; (2) Autonomy makes [tactical de-confliction | emergent response] decisions within [airborne limits]; (3) Crew retain override for [phase change | safety-critical decisions | manual intervention ≤ X seconds]. Define 'meaningful control' as [crew can perceive state via telemetry ≤ X Hz], [understand autonomy decision via telemetry ≤ Y Hz], [intervene within ≤ Z seconds]. Autonomy decisions not requiring crew approval: [coordinated avoidance, re-routing, speed adjustment]. Decisions requiring crew approval: [mission abort, geofence change].'

findings.finding_title: No data validation / data entry rules requirement—artefact quality not enforced
findings.what_is_missing: No requirement specifies validation rules for data entry or artefact upload. Examples: ConOps document must include sections A, B, C; SORA evidence must have risk matrix in format X; insurance certificate must be in PDF; geofence must be defined as polygon with [min X, max X, min Y, max Y]. Without validation rules, applicants can submit incomplete or non-conformant artefacts that system accepts but reviewers reject, creating rework and delay.
findings.where_expected: A cross-cutting NFR should define data validation for each artefact type. Examples: ConOps validation rule: 'ConOps document must be >= 10 pages, include sections for [nominal procedures, contingency procedures, crew roles, decision triggers], and define all acronyms on first use.' Insurance validation: 'Certificate must be PDF, from approved insurer, coverage >= £10M, exclusions documented, valid for [duration of trial + 1 year].'
findings.impact: Without validation rules, quality control is manual and subjective. Applicants receive inconsistent feedback on submission acceptability. Case officer spends time re-requesting missing information. Early validation catches errors before formal review, saving time and frustration.
findings.reasoning: REQ001 (F02 Completeness Check) requires validation but does not define validation rules beyond 'complete'. Flo's features analysis identifies validation as part of F02 but notes 'what constitutes complete is undefined'. Alexa's ambiguity analysis notes 'complete pack is undefined; no artefact enumeration.' Without explicit validation rules, system cannot automate completeness check.
findings.recommendation: Define explicit data validation requirement: 'As a system validator I want to enforce data entry rules so that submitted artefacts conform to CAA standards and reduce rework. Validation rules per artefact: (1) ConOps: [format=PDF, size>10 pages, must include sections: [Nominal Procedures, Contingencies, Crew Roles, Decision Trees], acronyms defined on first use]. (2) ODD: [format=structured table or spreadsheet, must define: [geographic bounds (lat/lon), altitude range, environmental limits (wind, visibility, temperature)]]. (3) SORA evidence: [must include JARUS SORA v2.5 risk matrix, hazard assessment, mitigation evidence per [structured table]]. (4) Insurance: [must be PDF from [approved insurer list], coverage amount >= £10M, exclusions documented, valid dates overlapping trial period]. (5) Geofence: [format=KML or [proprietary format], must be closed polygon, all points defined with [latitude, longitude, altitude, tolerance band]]. Validation: [automated via schema / manual checklist]. Feedback on failure: [specific violation reported to applicant].'

findings.finding_title: No Workbench assurance requirement—system itself is not tested / assured
findings.what_is_missing: The system (Workbench) that manages BVLOS authorization cases is not covered by requirements. System risks are: [bugs that corrupt case data], [false positive/negative completeness checks], [audit trail tampering], [unauthorized access]. Yet no requirement addresses: testing strategy for the system, version control, change management, backup/disaster recovery, or post-deployment monitoring. The Workbench itself could fail and invalidate all case authorizations.
findings.where_expected: A meta-requirement should address Workbench assurance: 'As a CAA IT manager I want the Workbench system to be assured so that case data integrity and decision defensibility are maintained. The system shall meet: [availability SLA 99.9% | redundancy and backup | incident response plan | version control for all configuration / rule changes | pre-deployment testing per [development standard] | post-deployment monitoring for [correctness, performance, security]].'
findings.impact: If Workbench fails (data corruption, security breach, availability loss), all pending cases are affected. Regulators lose case history and audit trail. In accident investigation, opposing counsel may argue authorization decision is not defensible because system integrity is questioned. Post-deployment bugs may silently change gate logic or allow unauthorized users to access classified evidence.
findings.reasoning: REQs focus on BVLOS trial authorization but not on the system that manages authorizations. No analyst explicitly mentioned Workbench assurance. However, this is a classic 'assurance of the tool' meta-problem: the tool itself (Workbench) must be assured with same rigor as the trial it manages.
findings.recommendation: Add meta-requirement: 'As a CAA system owner I want the Workbench system to be assured so that authorization decisions are legally defensible. Workbench shall meet: (1) [Availability]: SLA 99.9% uptime; failover to [backup system] within [5 minutes] of failure. (2) [Data integrity]: [nightly backup], [backup tested monthly], [disaster recovery plan tested annually]. (3) [Change control]: all configuration and rule changes tracked in [version control]; changes approved by [CAA manager + technical review] before deployment. (4) [Testing]: pre-deployment testing per [ISO/IEC 25010 software quality model], including [functional testing, integration testing, regression testing, performance testing]. (5) [Security]: access control per [role-based access control], [audit logging of all access], [encryption of data at rest and in transit]. (6) [Monitoring]: post-deployment monitoring for [system availability, performance (latency, throughput), security incidents (unauthorized access attempts), audit log integrity]. (7) [Incident response]: [on-call support team], [incident response plan], [post-incident review and lessons learned]. (8) [Documentation]: system design documentation, change log, known issues list maintained in [version control].'

findings.finding_title: REQ001: Null outcome - incomplete pack is received but handled silently
findings.outcome_description: Possibility that system receives an incomplete pack and takes NO ACTION: no rejection message, no feedback, no case opening. Pack is silently ingested and sits in queue indefinitely.
findings.triggering_scenario: applicant submits incomplete pack; system ingests it but completeness check is not performed automatically
findings.reasoning: If completeness check (F02) is not automatic, incomplete packs could be ingested and stalled. This is a null outcome (literally nothing happens to alert the applicant).
findings.recommendation: Ensure automatic completeness check on pack ingestion: within 5 minutes of receipt, completeness check is triggered and result is sent to applicant (ACCEPT with case ID, or REJECT with missing-item list and re-submission instructions).

findings.finding_title: REQ004: TDA coordination outcome is null if coordination fails silently
findings.outcome_description: REQ004 specifies 'I want a TDA and consultation package so that I can segregate the trial', but if TDA coordination request is sent to airspace authorities and NO RESPONSE is received (coordination stalls), the requirement is not met but no alert is generated.
findings.triggering_scenario: CAA airspace regulator requests TDA from airspace authority; request is sent but response is delayed/missing
findings.reasoning: If TDA coordination is asynchronous (request sent, response awaited), and response is not received, system could stall indefinitely. This is a null outcome (trial cannot proceed without TDA, but system doesn't escalate or warn).
findings.recommendation: Define TDA coordination outcome with timeout: if TDA response is not received within 30 days, system escalates (CAA manager is notified, trial authorization is held pending TDA, stakeholder is informed of delay). Define timeout and escalation procedure.

findings.finding_title: REQ006-REQ011: Conflicting outcomes - link loss handling vs contingency matrix coverage
findings.outcome_description: REQ006 (Link loss is survivable) implies autonomous behavior when C2 link is lost. REQ011 (Contingency matrix) implies pre-planned procedures for off-nominal states. But the contingency matrix may not cover all link-loss scenarios, creating a conflict: should system follow contingency matrix or fall back to autonomous RTL?
findings.triggering_scenario: C2 link is lost in scenario not covered by contingency matrix (e.g., link loss during swarm formation change)
findings.reasoning: REQ006 specifies link loss is 'survivable' (implicit outcome: swarm survives, no crash). REQ011 specifies contingency matrix covers off-nominal states. But link loss outcomes must be defined somewhere, either as pre-planned (REQ011) or as autonomous (REQ006). If not covered in contingency matrix, what is fallback?
findings.recommendation: Define link loss outcomes in contingency matrix: (1) contingency matrix must include specific rows for C2 link loss scenarios (loss during cruise, loss during approach, loss during formation change); (2) for each scenario, define response (autonomous RTL, hold position, swarm dissolution); (3) if actual link loss scenario is not in matrix (gap), system executes generic fallback (hold position + autonomous RTL to last known safe location); (4) matrix coverage is a gate criterion for REQ016 gate approval (Phase N gate cannot be approved if contingency matrix has gaps). Define matrix structure and fallback logic.

findings.finding_title: REQ007-REQ008: Unclassified outcome - DAA resolution vs swarm decision-making architecture priority
findings.outcome_description: REQ007 (DAA performance assures encounters resolved predictably) and REQ008 (Swarm decision-making architecture is assured) both influence how swarm responds to encounters. But the priority is unclear: if swarm decision-making logic conflicts with DAA logic (e.g., formation preservation vs. collision avoidance), which takes priority?
findings.triggering_scenario: encounter detected; DAA system recommends right turn to avoid intruder; swarm decision-making logic recommends tight formation, which would conflict with turn
findings.reasoning: Both REQ007 (DAA) and REQ008 (swarm coordination) influence swarm behavior during encounters. But they may conflict: collision avoidance may require breaking formation; maintaining formation may conflict with safe separation. The requirements do not specify which takes priority.
findings.recommendation: Define priority outcomes: (1) collision avoidance (DAA, REQ007) has ABSOLUTE priority over formation (swarm architecture, REQ008); (2) when encounter is detected, all swarm members are alerted and prioritize separation over formation maintenance; (3) formation re-establishment occurs AFTER encounter resolution; (4) swarm decision-making architecture must document this priority explicitly. Ensure architecture assurance (REQ008) includes this priority rule.

findings.finding_title: Unclear relationship between 'automatic containment' and 'independent termination'
findings.text_excerpt: REQ005 requires 'automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS'; REQ011 requires 'independent termination...so that the trial can be made safe in any off-nominal state'. Both are safety-critical; their relationship and scope division is undefined.
findings.interpretation_a: Containment prevents exit (hard boundary enforcement); termination ensures safe landing if containment fails
findings.interpretation_b: Both containment and termination are redundant measures; relationship undefined (are they independent? overlapping? hierarchical?)
findings.reasoning: Both requirements address aircraft safety in the event of loss of control or out-of-bounds flight, but they seem to operate at different layers. Containment is preventive (keep aircraft in bounds); termination is corrective (land safely if in-bounds control fails). However, the requirements do not clarify this hierarchy or their interaction.
findings.recommendation: Clarify the relationship: 'Containment and termination are two independent layers of flight safety: (1) Containment (REQ005) is the primary layer, enforcing [hard geofence] to prevent inadvertent exit. (2) Termination (REQ011) is the secondary layer, triggered if [containment fails | aircraft exits bounds | loss-of-signal timer expires | autonomy watchdog fails]. Each layer shall function independently. Verification: test both layers in isolation and in combination per [DO-178C].'

findings.finding_title: Undefined reference: 'the swarm'—composition and coordination not specified
findings.text_excerpt: Requirements frequently refer to 'the swarm', 'swarm de-confliction', 'swarm decision-making' without defining swarm composition, size, coordination mechanism, or distinction from 'formation' or 'multi-agent system'.
findings.interpretation_a: Swarm = 3-4 coordinated aircraft with wireless inter-vehicle comms; real-time cooperative de-confliction
findings.interpretation_b: Swarm = N aircraft (unbounded) with pre-planned flight paths; minimal runtime coordination
findings.reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'swarm' as unverified: 'Core concept but boundaries undefined. Minimum/maximum aircraft count? Coordination mechanism? Distinct from formation or multi-agent system?' Different interpretations imply different autonomy, communication, and failure-mode requirements. A 4-aircraft swarm with wireless comms has different assurance challenges than a 20-aircraft swarm with pre-planned routes.
findings.recommendation: Define swarm: 'For this trial, swarm shall mean [N = 2 to 5 | N = defined per phase gate] aircraft operating under [centralized | distributed] control with coordination via [wireless inter-vehicle link | pre-planned routes | hybrid model]. Swarm shall coordinate on: [collision avoidance, formation keeping, task allocation]. Swarm shall be distinct from formation flying (which is fixed geometry) and multi-agent system (which may lack real-time coordination).'

findings.finding_title: No regulator training / onboarding requirement—assessor competency not defined
findings.what_is_missing: No requirement specifies qualifications or training for CAA assessors (SORA assessor, DAA assessor, autonomy reviewer, human factors inspector, environmental assessor). Implied qualifications (expert in their domain) are never formalized. No requirement addresses: how are assessors trained on swarm-specific risks? How is JARUS SORA v2.5 expertise ensured? How are conflicts of interest managed?
findings.where_expected: A cross-cutting requirement should define assessor competency: 'Each CAA assessment role shall be performed by a person with: [domain expertise (education + experience)], [training on CAA processes and this trial], [no conflict of interest with applicant].' Example: 'SORA assessor shall have: [minimum X years aviation safety experience], [completion of JARUS SORA v2.5 training course], [independent of trial operator].'
findings.impact: Without assessor qualification requirement, assessment quality is inconsistent. New CAA staff may not understand swarm-specific risks. Assessment may be biased if assessor has financial relationship with applicant. Future trials may repeat mistakes from prior trials if lessons-learned are not formalized in training.
findings.reasoning: REQ010 specifies 'CAA human factors inspector' and other roles but does not define their qualifications. Flo's features analysis notes role definitions are implied but not explicit. No analyst mentioned assessor training as a gap, but absence of training requirement in a complex domain (BVLOS swarm autonomy) is notable.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'As a CAA training director I want to ensure assessor competency so that assessments are consistent and of high quality. For each assessment role, define: (1) SORA assessor: [minimum 5 years aviation safety experience, completion of JARUS SORA v2.5 course (annually updated), certification on swarm-specific risk assessment (new internal training)]. (2) DAA assessor: [minimum 3 years DAA or collision avoidance experience, certification on DO-365 and F3442 standards, swarm encounter scenarios (new internal training)]. (3) Autonomy reviewer: [minimum 5 years autonomous systems experience, training on distributed decision-making and emergent behavior analysis]. (4) Environmental assessor: [certification in environmental impact assessment, noise modeling, wildlife impact analysis]. (5) Conflict of interest: assessor cannot work for / have financial interest in / be related to applicant. Assessor training shall be updated annually to reflect lessons-learned from completed trials.'

findings.finding_title: No inter-version clause-library migration requirement—how old cases are re-assessed under new CAA guidance
findings.what_is_missing: BVLOS swarm trials may extend over 2-3 years. During that time, CAA guidance (CAP 722H, CAP 1616) may be updated, causing interpretation changes or new requirements. No requirement specifies how ongoing authorizations handle guidance updates: (1) Are old authorizations grandfathered under prior guidance? (2) Must applicants re-assess under new guidance? (3) If new guidance imposes stricter safety standards, can ongoing trials be required to comply? How are conflicts resolved?
findings.where_expected: A requirement should address clause versioning: 'As a CAA case officer I want regulatory clause versions to be pinned to case authorization so that future re-assessment is clear when guidance changes. When CAA guidance (CAP 722H, etc.) is updated, the system shall: [1] mark applicable cases for re-assessment, [2] notify applicant of new guidance, [3] set timeline for re-assessment per [policy], [4] generate re-assessment report showing compliance with new guidance.'
findings.impact: Without guidance versioning, applicant may be uncertain whether ongoing trial is still compliant after CAA updates guidance. Regulator may be liable if an incident occurs and new guidance would have prevented it. Case file loses clarity about which guidance version applied to the authorization decision.
findings.reasoning: Flo's features analysis identifies F23 (Clause-Library Version Pinning & Management) as a hidden feature but notes it is 'not explicitly requested; implied by need for repeatable regulatory interpretation.' No REQ explicitly addresses version management for guidance documents.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'As a CAA case officer I want clause library versions pinned to authorization so that case interpretation is reproducible and re-assessment procedures are clear. System shall: (1) Record the version/date of all regulatory clauses (CAP 722H, CAP 1616, etc.) at time of authorization. (2) When a clause is updated, mark all cases pinned to prior version as [eligible for re-assessment]. (3) Notification process: [applicant notified within 30 days of clause update], offered choice to [maintain authorization under prior clause version OR re-assess under new version]. (4) Re-assessment timeline: [60 days] for material changes, [annual review] for minor updates. (5) Conflict resolution: if new guidance imposes stricter safety requirements, phased in per [policy] (e.g., new clause applies to [ongoing trials or new trials only]). (6) Audit trail: case file shows which clause version was used for authorization decision.'

findings.finding_title: No requirement for case appeal / re-opening—how denied authorizations can be challenged
findings.what_is_missing: If CAA denies authorization (insufficient evidence, unacceptable risk, non-compliance with clause), no requirement specifies applicant's right to appeal or re-submit. Can applicant submit new evidence and request re-review? Is there a deadline for re-submission? Is appeal to independent body (not the original assessor) possible? Without appeal mechanism, applicant's only option is legal challenge (court action), which is expensive and slow.
findings.where_expected: A feature should address appeal/re-opening: 'As an applicant I want to appeal a rejected authorization or re-open a case with new evidence so that I can challenge regulatory decisions and remediate issues identified in rejection.'
findings.impact: Without appeal mechanism, applicant has no path forward after rejection except litigation. Regulator has finality but may make errors (misunderstand evidence, apply wrong standard). Industry (potential BVLOS operators) may be discouraged if they perceive regulatory decisions are unfair or unchangeable.
findings.reasoning: No analyst explicitly mentioned appeal mechanism. REQ016 (phased gates) includes reversal (phase can be rolled back) but this is different from appeal (challenging original decision, not rolling back progression). No REQ addresses post-denial remediation.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'As an applicant I want to appeal a rejected authorization so that I can remediate issues and resubmit without restarting the entire process. Appeal process: (1) Rejection decision includes [specific findings], [reasons for denial], [opportunities to remediate]. (2) Applicant has [60 days] to file [appeal request with new evidence | re-submission plan] addressing specific findings. (3) Re-review conducted by [different assessor | assessor + independent expert], timeline [30 days]. (4) Re-review may result in [APPROVED (remediation successful)], [CONDITIONAL (approved if applicant meets conditions)], or [DENIED (no remediation possible)]. (5) Final decision is documented in case file with appeal history. (6) Applicant may request [mediation | independent panel review] if still disagreeing with re-review decision.'

findings.finding_title: No cross-tool interoperability requirement—Workbench integration with airspace change tools, AAIB, Service Inquiry undefined
findings.what_is_missing: BVLOS trials may involve multiple regulatory tools: [TDA coordination with airspace change system], [post-incident reporting to AAIB or Service Inquiry], [case data export to regulatory audit system]. No requirement specifies how Workbench integrates with these tools or exchanges data. Example: does TDA approval in airspace system automatically update case status in Workbench? Does incident investigation in AAIB system reference case authorization from Workbench?
findings.where_expected: A cross-cutting requirement should address interoperability: 'As a CAA systems administrator I want Workbench to integrate with [airspace change system | AAIB | audit system] so that trial authorization and oversight are coordinated across systems. Integration shall include: [data exchange format (e.g., XML, API)], [synchronization frequency], [conflict resolution if systems disagree].'
findings.impact: Without interoperability, Workbench is an isolated system. Data is manually re-entered into other systems, creating inconsistency and error. Regulators must check multiple systems to understand full trial status. Incidents may not be linked back to originating authorization (AAIB investigates incident but doesn't have Workbench context).
findings.reasoning: REQ004 (TDA coordination) and REQ012 (MOR/incident reporting) imply integration with external systems but don't specify mechanism. Flo's features analysis notes no hidden features for interoperability. No analyst mentioned cross-tool integration.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'As a CAA systems administrator I want Workbench to integrate with external regulatory systems so that trial lifecycle is coordinated. Integration points: (1) TDA / airspace change system: [when TDA approved in airspace system, status automatically updates in Workbench]. (2) AAIB incident system: [when incident reported to AAIB, Workbench case is linked and marked with incident reference]. (3) CAA audit system: [Workbench exports case summary (applicant, authorization date, SAIL/OSO, conditions) for audit trail]. (4) Crew licensing system: [crew credentials verified against CAA license database; updates to crew credentials trigger case review if competency changes]. Data format: [JSON API | XML / EDI], frequency: [real-time | daily batch sync], conflict resolution: [flag inconsistencies for manual review].'

findings.finding_title: 'Defensibly' and 'assure' use passive voice, obscuring responsibility
findings.current_usage: REQ002: 'so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly'; REQ008: 'so that I can assure what the swarm will do'
findings.proposed_definition: These verbs are used in passive goal statements without specifying who holds responsibility for the outcome or what authority the assessor actually has.
findings.reasoning: Both terms use passive construction: 'defensibly' and 'assure' describe outcomes the assessor wants, but do not specify who is responsible if the outcome is not achieved. Is the assessor responsible for quality? Or is the trial operator responsible for providing evidence? This creates accountability ambiguity in case of disputes.
findings.recommendation: Reword requirements to clarify accountability: Instead of 'I want swarm-aware SORA evidence so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly', rephrase as: 'I want the trial operator to provide swarm-aware SORA evidence, from which I will derive SAIL and OSO requirements; I am responsible for ensuring these requirements are set defensibly and documented.' Similarly, rephrase REQ008 to clarify: 'I am responsible for assuring the swarm decision-making architecture; the trial operator must provide evidence of nominal and emergent behaviors so that I can make this assurance statement.'
